analogy: Think of "The Age of Industrialisation" through the chapter's own opening images: 79 The Age of Industrialisation T h e A g e o f I n d us t r i a l isa t i o n Chapter IV The Age of Industrialisation The Age of Industrialisation In 1900, a popular music publisher E.T. Paull... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
The Age of Industrialisation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 79 The Age of Industrialisation T h e A g e o f I n d us t r i a l isa t i o n Chapter IV The Age of Industrialisation The Age of Industrialisation In 1900, a popular music publisher E.T.

The text explains that paull produced a music book that had a picture on the cover page announcing the ‘Dawn of the Century’ (Fig.

The text explains that as you can see from the illustration, at the centre of the picture is a goddess-like figure, the angel of progress, bearing the flag of the new century.

The text explains that she is gently perched on a wheel with wings, symbolising time.

The text explains that floating about, behind her, are the signs of progress: railway, camera, machines, printing press and factory.

The text explains that this glorification of machines and technology is even more marked in a picture which appeared on the pages of a trade magazine over a hundred years ago (Fig.

The text explains that the one at the top is Aladdin from the Orient who built a beautiful palace with his New words Orient – The countries to the east of the Mediterranean, usually referring to Asia.

The text explains that the term arises out of a western viewpoint that sees this region as pre- modern, traditional and mysterious Fig.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 80 Give two examples where modern development that is associated with progress has led to problems.

The text explains that you may like to think of areas related to environmental issues, nuclear weapons or disease.

The text explains that the one at the bottom is the modern mechanic, who with his modern tools weaves a new magic: builds bridges, ships, towers and high-rise buildings.

The text explains that aladdin is shown as representing the East and the past, the mechanic stands for the West and modernity.

The text explains that these images offer us a triumphant account of the modern world.

The text explains that within this account the modern world is associated with rapid technological change and innovations, machines and factories, railways and steamships.

The text explains that the history of industrialisation thus becomes simply a story of development, and the modern age appears as a wonderful time of technological progress.

The text explains that these images and associations have now become part of popular imagination.

The text explains that cottagers and poor peasants who had earlier depended on common lands for their survival, gathering their firewood, berries, vegetables, hay and straw, had to now look for alternative sources of income.

The text explains that it also allowed them a fuller use of their family labour resources.

The text explains that source Source A A range of products could be produced only with hand labour.

The text explains that source Source B New words Spinning Jenny – Devised by James Hargreaves in 1764, this machine speeded up the spinning process and reduced labour demand.

The text explains that first: the Company tried to eliminate the existing traders and brokers connected with the cloth trade, and establish a more direct control over the weaver.

The text explains that source Source C Reporting on the Koshtis, a community of weavers, the Census Report of Central Provinces stated: ‘The Koshtis, like the weavers of the finer kinds of cloth in other parts of India, have fallen upon evil times.

The text explains that 95 The Age of Industrialisation commercial groups, but they were not directly involved in external trade.

The text explains that source Source E Bhai Bhosle, a trade unionist of Bombay, recollected his childhood in the 1930s and 1940s: ‘In those days, the shift was 10 hours – from 5 pm to 3 am – terrible working hours.

The text explains that by 1941, over 35 per cent of handlooms in India were fitted with fly shuttles: in regions like Travancore, Madras, Mysore, Cochin, Bengal the proportion was 70 to 80 per cent.

The text explains that how did the East India Company procure regular supplies of cotton and silk textiles from Indian weavers?

The text explains that imagine that you have been asked to write an article for an encyclopaedia on Britain and the history of cotton.

The text explains that write your piece using information from the entire chapter.

The text explains that why did industrial production in India increase during the First World War?

The text explains that select any one industry in your region and find out its history.

The text explains that how are the products advertised and marketed?

The text explains that try and talk to the employers and some workers to get their views about the industry’s history.

The text explains that explain the following: a) Women workers in Britain attacked the Spinning Jenny. b) In the seventeenth century merchants from towns in Europe began employing peasants and artisans within the villages. c) The port of Surat declined by the end of the eighteenth century. d) The East India Company appointed gomasthas to supervise weavers in India.

The text explains that write True or False against each statement: a) At the end of the nineteenth century, 80 per cent of the total workforce in Europe was employed in the technologically advanced industrial sector. b) The international market for fine textiles was dominated by India till the eighteenth century. c) The American Civil War resulted in the reduction of cotton exports from India. d) The introduction of the fly shuttle enabled handloom workers to improve their productivity.

The text explains that explain what is meant by proto-industrialisation.

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 4: The Age of Industrialisation

schemes.name: They
schemes.point: They pressurised the government to impose import duties on cotton textiles so that Manchester goods could sell in Britain without facing any competition from outside. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch4 pg 13)

schemes.name: They
schemes.point: They established tea and coffee plantations, acquiring land at cheap rates from the colonial government; and they invested in mining, indigo and jute. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch4 pg 18)

schemes.name: Industrial
schemes.point: Industrial groups organised themselves to protect their collective interests, pressurising the government to increase tariff protection and grant other concessions. (NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II Ch4 pg 18)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1 Before the Industrial Revolution and 1.1 The Coming Up of the Factory as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Age of Industrialisation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Age of Industrialisation through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1 Before the Industrial Revolution and 1.1 The Coming Up of the Factory to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
